rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Scope: 140 failed OTIF orders with combined order value of AED 1,510,049.

RANKED DRIVERS BY CASH EXPOSURE

Rank one — supplier: AED 636,102 (42% of total). Supplier lateness clustered across the month, with PO overdue windows ranging from a handful of days to nearly a fortnight. In the majority of pure-supplier cases the late PO quantity alone was sufficient to have closed the pick shortfall had it arrived on time. In a subset of orders both a demand surge and a late PO were present; where the PO quantity exceeded the shortfall, supplier lateness was adjudicated the dominant cause. Recommended action: implement a tiered PO-confirmation and expedite protocol — require binding ship-confirmation at the midpoint of each lead-time window and trigger an automatic escalation to an alternative approved source when a supplier misses that checkpoint.

Rank two — demand: AED 501,315 (33% of total). Widespread under-forecasting drove stock depletion at pick across multiple lanes and categories, with forecast errors frequently exceeding thirty percent. In roughly half the demand-attributed orders a concurrent supplier delay was also present, but the forecast miss alone was sufficient to explain the inventory gap. Recommended action: introduce a rolling demand-signal refresh that incorporates point-of-sale and channel-inventory data at a weekly cadence, and set safety-stock buffers calibrated to the observed forecast-error distribution rather than a fixed percentage.

Rank three — warehouse: AED 195,477 (13% of total). Transit overages on short-SLA lanes were the dominant failure mode; a subset of these orders also carried a warehouse dispatch delay that consumed the available transit buffer before the carrier even collected the shipment. Recommended action: renegotiate carrier SLA terms on the highest-value lanes and introduce a real-time track-and-trace alert that flags any shipment exceeding fifty percent of its SLA window so that proactive customer communication and re-routing options can be activated.

Rank four — logistics: AED 177,154 (12% of total). Two distinct warehouse failure modes were identified: dispatch delays where stock was present but the order was held beyond the pick-ready date, and pure pick-short events where on-hand inventory was sufficient yet fewer units were physically picked than were available. Recommended action: separate the two root causes operationally — for dispatch delays, enforce a same-shift dispatch-cut-off rule with supervisor sign-off for any exception; for pick shorts, conduct a slotting and pick-path audit to identify the locations generating systematic under-picks and retrain the affected teams.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED

For the contested orders where both a demand surge and a supplier delay were simultaneously present, the adjudication relied on whether the PO quantity alone exceeded the shortfall and on the relative magnitude of the forecast error; the true counterfactual — what inventory would have been available had either factor been resolved in isolation — could not be determined from the signals provided. Additionally, no cost-of-delay or margin data were available, so the cash figures reflect order value rather than net margin impact, and the true profit exposure may differ materially across product categories.
